original_abstract: The electrochemical oxidation of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) to 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA) represents a sustainable pathway for producing bio-based polymers, yet the process is frequently complicated by the inherent instability of HMF in the alkaline media required for efficient catalysis. In this study, we investigate the alkaline degradation of HMF and demonstrate that it undergoes a selective Cannizzaro disproportionation, forming 5-hydroxymethyl-2-furancarboxylic acid (HMFCA) and dihydroxymethylfuran (DHMF). Unlike HMF, which readily polymerizes into insoluble humins at high pH, these Cannizzaro products remain stable in strongly alkaline solutions even at elevated temperatures. We show that both HMFCA and DHMF can be effectively electrooxidized to FDCA with high faradaic efficiencies (~98%) using transition-metal-based electrodes. By leveraging the stability of these intermediates, we developed an "indirect" electrochemical route that enables the processing of HMF at significantly higher substrate and base concentrations than previously reported. This approach mitigates humin formation and allows for technical-scale current densities exceeding 1 A cm⁻², substantially enhancing the space–time yield and commercial viability of biomass-derived plastic precursor production.
humanized_abstract: The electrochemical oxidation of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) into 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA) has been proposed as a new sustainable route for the synthesis of bio-based polymers. However, the transformation is often hampered by the instability of HMF in alkaline conditions that are necessary for the functioning of the catalysts usually employed. Herein we report the alkaline degradation of HMF and observe a selective Cannizzaro disproportionation into 5-hydroxymethyl-2-furancarboxylic acid (HMFCA) and dihydroxymethylfuran (DHMF). In contrast with HMF, the Cannizzaro products are stable in strongly alkaline solution up to 80 °C. Both HMFCA and DHMF are then directly electrooxidized into FDCA with a high faradaic efficiency of ca. 98% at transition metal based working electrodes. Based on these results, an “indirect” electrochemical route that allows the transformation of HMF at a much higher concentration than the previously reported and with a higher substrate and base concentration, thus reducing the accumulation of humins and affording higher space–time yields at technical scale by enabling current densities above 1 A cm⁻².
